nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reefs, kelp beds, sandy flats, deep crevices and small caves; benthic species typically found at depths of 2–11 m, with juveniles occupying deeper sandy flats to 150 m

diet.notes: Horn Sharks have been documented feeding primarily on hard-shelled invertebrates including sea urchins, crustaceans, mollusks, and abalone, using pointed front teeth to grasp prey and molariform rear teeth for grinding. They tend to opportunistically take small bony fishes as well. In aquaria, they have generally been maintained on vitamin-enriched squid, fresh shrimp, scallop, and marine fish flesh offered 2–3 times per week. Dim lighting during feeding is generally advised to encourage natural nocturnal feeding behavior.

summary: A robust, cool-water Pacific shark generally considered suitable only for expert aquarists with very large systems (500+ gal). Horn Sharks tend to be nocturnal bottom-dwellers that have been reported to adapt well to captivity when provided with deep sand beds and ample rocky shelters.
careNotes:
The Horn Shark (Heterodontus francisci) is a small bottom-dwelling shark native to the cool waters of the eastern Pacific. Aquarium husbandry requires cold-water conditions — generally advised to keep at 16–24°C (60–75°F). Higher temperatures have been associated with stress and poor health.

This species has been reported to reach about 1 m total length in the wild; aquarium specimens typically max out smaller. A very large tank with sandy substrate and rockwork crevices is generally advised. Paired dorsal-fin spines can injure unwary handlers.

Diet is invertebrate-focused — sea urchins, crustaceans, and molluscs are commonly cited; in captivity offer a variety of meaty foods. Nocturnal habits are common; the fish tends to rest under ledges during the day.

Wild lifespan has been reported at 12 years (FishBase); one unverified record cites up to 25 years.
breedingNotes: Horn Sharks are oviparous and have been bred in public aquarium settings, though captive breeding in home aquaria is considered very difficult. Mating has been observed to occur around December–January, with egg deposition in February–April. Females have been reported to lay eggs in pairs every 11–14 days, producing up to approximately 24 eggs per season. Eggs have been documented requiring 6–10 months to hatch. Males reach sexual maturity at approximately 56–61 cm; females at approximately 58 cm. Captive breeding is generally considered advanced to expert level and has primarily been achieved in institutional public aquarium settings.

fish.breedingNotes: Horn Sharks are oviparous and have been bred in public aquarium settings; captive breeding in home aquaria has been reported but is generally considered an advanced undertaking. Eggs have been known to require 6–10 months to hatch; females tend to place eggs in rocky crevices.
